FACTS: Landlord served an immediate 3-day notice to quit for an alleged uncorrected lease violation without first providing a distinct warning notice and a separate opportunity to cure.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-805 (At-Fault Eviction Notice Deficiency): Client Kamala Khan faced eviction after her landlord served an immediate three-day notice to quit, citing an unauthorized pet on the premises (intake notes). The property is an older apartment building fully covered by the Tenant Protection Act's just cause provisions (memo tracking). Under Section 1946.2(c), if an at-fault violation is curable, the landlord cannot issue an absolute notice to quit without first providing a preliminary notice of violation and a reasonable opportunity to correct the problem. Outcome: We filed an answer to the unlawful detainer highlighting the lack of a separate, prior opportunity to cure; the court sustained our defense and dismissed the landlord's lawsuit for failure to meet the statutory pre-notice prerequisites (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [No-Fault Notice Disclosure], [Relocation Option Election], [Damages Clawback Defense]. STATUTES: [Cal. Civ. Code § 1946.2(d)]. FACTS: Landlord issued a no-fault notice to terminate a tenancy for owner move-in but failed to state whether they would pay relocation funds or waive the final month's rent.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-822 (No-Fault Relocation Option Disclosure Defect): Client Doreen Green received a 60-day notice terminating her multi-year tenancy so the landlord's son could occupy the unit (intake notes). While the notice stated the reason was an owner-intent move-in, it completely omitted the mandatory statutory text indicating how the landlord would fulfill the relocation assistance obligation (memo tracking). The Tenant Protection Act strictly requires the termination notice itself to outline the landlord's explicit election between a direct one-month rent payment or a written waiver of the final month's rent. Outcome: We notified the landlord's counsel of the fatal facial defect in the notice; the landlord voluntarily dismissed their planned unlawful detainer action to avoid an adverse judgment and re-served a compliant notice (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Three-Day Notice Day Counting], [Court Holiday Exclusion], [Premature Unlawful Detainer Filing]. STATUTES: [Cal. Civ. Code § 1946.2], [Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 12]. FACTS: Landlord served a 3-day notice to pay or quit on the Friday before Labor Day and counted Saturday, Sunday, and Monday as part of the compliance period.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-840 (Three-Day Notice Holiday Miscalculation): Client Peter Quill was served a three-day notice to pay rent or quit on the Friday directly preceding Labor Day weekend (intake notes). The landlord counted Saturday, Sunday, and the Monday federal holiday as the three days and filed an unlawful detainer lawsuit on Tuesday morning (memo tracking). For curable three-day notices, California law strictly dictates that counting begins on the first day that is not a weekend or court holiday; therefore, day one was Tuesday, day two was Wednesday, and day three was Thursday. Outcome: We filed a motion to quash the summons on the grounds that the eviction lawsuit was filed prematurely before the tenant's statutory compliance period had expired; the court granted the motion and quashed the suit (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Substituted Service Compliance], [Nail and Mail Mailing Prong], [Notice Period Commencement]. STATUTES: [Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 1162]. FACTS: Landlord posted a three-day notice on the front door of a tenant's apartment but completely forgot to mail a concurrent copy to the address.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-865 (Service of Process Posting and Mailing Defect): Client Carol Danvers discovered a three-day notice to quit tacked to her front door after returning home from work (intake notes). The landlord immediately filed an eviction action exactly three days later without ever mailing a secondary copy of the notice to her residence (memo tracking). Under the statutory 'posting and mailing' procedure, service is legally incomplete until both prongs are satisfied, and the compliance clock cannot begin until the day after both requirements are met. Outcome: We presented proof at the summary trial that no copy was ever delivered via the U.S. Postal Service; the judge ruled the service was invalid, stripping the court of jurisdiction and resulting in a judgment for the tenant (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Summary Eviction Self-Help], [Utility Interruption Liability], [Statutory Daily Penalties]. STATUTES: [Cal. Civ. Code § 789.3]. FACTS: Landlord bypassed the court system entirely, cut off water and electricity to force an alleged holdover tenant out, and changed the exterior door locks.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-890 (Illegal Landlord Self-Help and Utility Disconnection): Client Scott Lang remained in possession of his apartment following a disputed lease termination notice (intake notes). Rather than filing an unlawful detainer summary lawsuit as mandated by the court-administered eviction process, the landlord shut off the building's water supply, disconnected Scott's electricity, and changed the deadbolt locks on the front door (memo tracking). California law stringently prohibits any self-help measures, making landlords liable for actual damages plus punitive fines of up to $100 per day for intentional utility disruption. Outcome: We obtained an emergency temporary restraining order forcing the immediate restoration of utilities and utility access, and secured a settlement awarding Scott $4,500 in statutory daily penalties and moving cost damages (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Ten Court Day Response Deadline], [Affirmative Defense Pleading], [Default Judgment Avoidance]. STATUTES: [Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 1167]. FACTS: Tenant was served an unlawful detainer complaint but delayed filing a response, narrowly escaping a default judgment by submitting a written answer on the tenth court day.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-911 (Expedited Unlawful Detainer Response): Client Matt Murdock sought urgent representation after receiving an unlawful detainer summons and complaint (intake notes). Due to recent statutory revisions, the deadline to file an answer is restricted to ten court days, excluding weekends and judicial holidays, making immediate pleading imperative to avoid a default judgment (memo tracking). We prepared a comprehensive written answer containing essential affirmative defenses, specifically noting that the landlord's underlying three-day notice demanded an incorrect and inflated rent amount. Outcome: The answer was timely filed with the Clerk of Court on the tenth court day, successfully blocking the landlord's default application and preserving the tenant's right to contest the eviction at trial (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Answer Proof of Service], [Non-Party Adult Server Requirement], [Default Judgment Technical Defeat]. STATUTES: [Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 1013a]. FACTS: Landlord attempted to secure a default judgment by claiming they never received the tenant's answer, despite it being served by mail via an independent adult.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-928 (Answer Service and Proof Verification): Client Foggy Nelson filed a timely answer to an eviction lawsuit but faced a default risk when the landlord claimed non-receipt of the document (intake notes). Under California civil procedure, a tenant must ensure that a copy of the written answer is served upon the landlord's attorney by an individual over the age of 18 who is not a party to the litigation (memo tracking). This third-party server must complete and execute a formal Proof of Service form, which is subsequently filed with the Clerk of the Court. Outcome: We produced the timestamped Proof of Service executed by the independent server; the court rejected the landlord's default motion, scheduling the matter for a standard trial (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Five-Day Discovery Notice], [Pre-Trial Discovery Completion Deadline], [Expedited Trial Discovery Prep]. STATUTES: [Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 2030.260]. FACTS: Landlord refused to respond to a tenant's document requests, claiming the standard 30-day civil discovery window applied instead of the summary eviction timeline.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-942 (Summary Eviction Discovery Enforcement): Client Luke Cage required expedited discovery to obtain property maintenance records demonstrating a major breach of the warranty of habitability (intake notes). Because unlawful detainer actions are summary proceedings, the discovery process operates under compressed timelines requiring a minimum of five days' notice for personal delivery or ten days if served by mail (memo tracking). Furthermore, all discovery mechanisms must be completed no later than five days before the scheduled trial date. Outcome: We personally served the landlord's counsel with requests for production; when they objected based on standard civil timelines, we filed an immediate motion to compel under the summary rules, forcing the production of the critical maintenance logs prior to the trial deadline (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Ex Parte Application for Stay], [Hardship Execution Delay], [Writ of Possession Postponement]. STATUTES: [Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 1176]. FACTS: Tenant lost an eviction trial due to a technical lease breach and faced an immediate lockout, risking extreme hardship to her bedridden family member.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-966 (Eviction Judgment Stay of Execution): Client Jessica Jones lost an unlawful detainer trial and was faced with an impending sheriff's lockout following the issuance of a writ of possession (intake notes). We prepared an urgent ex parte application for a stay of execution, supported by a detailed written declaration executed under penalty of perjury outlining severe medical hardships if an immediate relocation was forced (memo tracking). The court possesses equitable discretion to temporarily delay eviction execution, conditioned upon the tenant paying prospective rent for the exact duration of the stay. Outcome: The judge granted the ex parte application, staying the sheriff's lockout for 21 days to facilitate a safe, orderly relocation, while explicitly noting no past-due rent was required to secure this temporary relief (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Writ of Possession Five-Day Window], [Forcible Sheriff Eviction], [Personal Property Storage Duty]. STATUTES: [Cal. Civ. Code § 1951.3]. FACTS: Sheriff executed a writ of possession and locked out a tenant who left behind furniture; the landlord threatened to throw the items into the street immediately.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-989 (Post-Eviction Property Reclamation): Client Danny Rand was forcibly evicted by the sheriff after failing to vacate within five days of being served a writ of possession and notice to vacate (intake notes). The landlord locked the unit containing Danny's remaining personal belongings and demanded immediate payment of all back rent before releasing the property (memo tracking). Under California law, a landlord must exercise reasonable care and store a tenant's left-behind property for at least 15 days, and cannot condition the release of personal belongings on the payment of past-due court judgments, but only on reasonable storage and moving costs. Outcome: We sent a formal demand letter outlining the statutory property storage mandates; the landlord complied, accepted a calculated daily rental value for the storage days, and released the items safely to the client (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Default Judgment Vacate Window], [Excusable Neglect Pleading], [Proposed Answer Plausibility]. STATUTES: [Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 473(b)]. FACTS: Tenant failed to answer an eviction complaint due to sudden medical incapacitation; landlord secured a default writ while tenant was hospitalized.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-1011 (Vacating Default Eviction Judgment): Client Matt Murdock sought urgent relief after discovering that a default judgment and a writ of possession had been entered against him while he was hospitalized with a severe illness (intake notes). Because he was completely incapacitated during the ten court-day response window, we initiated an expedited proceeding under Section 473(b) to vacate the default based on excusable neglect (memo tracking). As required by California summary procedure, we accompanied the motion with a verified 'proposed answer' demonstrating a meritorious defense—specifically, that the landlord failed to properly serve the underlying notice. Outcome: The court found satisfactory excuse, set aside the default judgment, and ordered the clerk to file the proposed answer, successfully halting the impending sheriff's lockout (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Automatic Stay Relief Lift], [Pre-Petition Eviction Judgment], [Bad Faith Bankruptcy Sanctions]. STATUTES: [11 U.S.C. § 362]. FACTS: Tenant filed a skeletal bankruptcy petition solely to block a pre-existing eviction judgment; landlord petitioned the federal court to lift the stay.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-1034 (Bankruptcy Automatic Stay Overrule): We represented landlord Foggy Nelson regarding an eviction action against a tenant who filed a pro se bankruptcy petition on the eve of a scheduled sheriff's lockout (intake notes). The tenant had no other outstanding debts and filed no financial schedules, utilizing the filing purely as a delay tactic (memo tracking). Because the unlawful detainer judgment had been obtained prior to the bankruptcy petition, the automatic stay's protection was highly restricted. Outcome: We filed a motion for relief from the automatic stay in federal bankruptcy court; the judge ruled the petition was filed in bad faith solely to frustrate the summary state court process, lifted the stay immediately, and permitted the sheriff to proceed with the execution of the writ of possession (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Six-Month Retaliation Presumption], [Repair and Deduct Exercise], [Pretextual Motive Rebuttal]. STATUTES: [Cal. Civ. Code § 1942.5(a)]. FACTS: Landlord served a 30-day notice to quit exactly two months after the tenant utilized the statutory repair-and-deduct remedy for a broken heater.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-1055 (Retaliatory Eviction Defense Enforcement): Client Jessica Jones faced an eviction lawsuit after her landlord issued a notice to terminate her periodic tenancy (intake notes). Two months prior, Jessica had formally executed a 'repair and deduct' remedy after the landlord repeatedly ignored her written requests to fix a broken water heater (memo tracking). Under Section 1942.5(a), if a landlord initiates eviction actions within six months of a tenant exercising such a right, the law presumes a retaliatory motive, shifting the burden onto the landlord to prove a good-faith, non-pretextual reason. Outcome: The landlord failed to establish a credible, alternative economic justification at trial; the court ruled the eviction was unlawfully retaliatory and entered judgment for Jessica, awarding her $2,000 in statutory damages (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Discriminatory Retaliation Motive], [Fair Housing Act Defense], [Civil Rights Department Liaison]. STATUTES: [Cal. Gov. Code § 12955]. FACTS: Landlord targeted an active tenant organizer for eviction, using a minor noise complaint as a pretext to disguise a discriminatory animus.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-1078 (Retaliatory Discrimination and Fair Housing): Client Luke Cage was served with an unlawful detainer complaint after he publicly organized a building-wide tenants' association to protest unsafe living conditions (intake notes). The landlord claimed the eviction was based on a single, minor noise incident, but internal communications revealed a dominant purpose to punish Luke for his advocacy (memo tracking). California's Fair Employment and Housing Act provides an absolute affirmative defense if an eviction is driven by a desire to suppress protected tenant organizing or housing rights activities. Outcome: We presented clear proof of the landlord's retaliatory animus at trial; the court deemed the noise violation a mere pretext, ruled the eviction discriminatory, and dismissed the action with prejudice (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Legal Document Assistant Limits], [Unauthorized Practice of Law], [UDA Contract Cancellation]. STATUTES: [Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 6400]. FACTS: Tenant paid an unregulated 'eviction defense clinic' an upfront fee, only to discover they were unregistered assistants who filled out forms incorrectly.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-1102 (Unregulated Eviction Clinic Fraud): Client Danny Rand engaged our firm after previously hiring a for-profit 'eviction defense clinic' that falsely promised a guaranteed trial victory (intake notes). The clinic operators, who were merely non-lawyer Unlawful Detainer Assistants (UDAs), charged heavy upfront fees and provided flawed legal advice, nearly causing a default judgment (memo tracking). By law, UDAs cannot provide legal counsel, must be formally registered with the County Clerk, and their contracts are subject to a strict 24-hour consumer cancellation window. Outcome: We stepped in, corrected the defective court filings, and issued a formal statutory notice rescinding the UDA contract, forcing the clinic to return Danny's upfront fees (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Legal Document Assistant Limits], [Registration Disclosure Duty], [UDA Fraud Prevention]. STATUTES: [Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 6400]. FACTS: Tenant paid a 'paralegal service' to prepare a motion to quash service, but the service failed to disclose mandatory registration data, indicating an illegal operation.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-1215 (Defective Document Assistant Filings): Client Matt Murdock requested intervention after hiring a self-proclaimed 'paralegal service' to handle his summary responses (intake notes). The company drafted a defective Motion to Quash Service of Summons but refused to provide county registration numbers or sign as a registered Legal Document Assistant (LDA) or Unlawful Detainer Assistant (UDA) (memo tracking). Under California law, paralegals are strictly forbidden from practicing law independently or filing documents without direct attorney supervision, and authentic UDAs must explicitly disclose their valid county registration details on all pleadings. Outcome: We took over the case, corrected the technical errors, and submitted a regulatory report against the fraudulent non-attorney service for the unauthorized practice of law (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Mediation Fee Structuring], [Voluntary Dispute Resolution], [Non-Binding Settlement Facilitation]. STATUTES: [Cal. Evid. Code § 1119]. FACTS: Parties paused an active eviction trial to try courthouse mediation, successfully engineering a voluntary move-out timetable without a court judgment.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-1239 (Courthouse Mediation Settlement): Client Foggy Nelson was embroiled in an eviction dispute over alleged unauthorized occupants (intake notes). Prior to trial, we elected to utilize the county-funded alternative dispute resolution program, which provides neutral mediators right at the courthouse to facilitate a voluntary compromise (memo tracking). Unlike a formal arbitrator, the mediator has no power to impose a binding ruling, but rather helps both sides coordinate an acceptable middle ground to escape litigation risks. Outcome: The parties formulated a voluntary settlement agreement where the tenant agreed to a structured transition, avoiding an adverse trial judgment and completely saving both parties from severe litigation fees (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Prejudgment Claim Intervention], [Unnamed Occupant Eviction Safeguard], [Writ Protection Entry]. STATUTES: [Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 415.46]. FACTS: Unnamed occupant learned that the main tenant faced an eviction lawsuit, and filed a statutory intervention form to secure an individual hearing before the sheriff's lockout.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-1262 (Unnamed Occupant Possession Claim): Client Jessica Jones had been residing in an apartment as a roommate but was never officially added to the formal written lease agreement (intake notes). When the landlord filed an unlawful detainer action against the primary tenant, they failed to serve a Prejudgment Claim of Right to Possession on the unnamed occupants at the unit (memo tracking). Because she was omitted from the original summons and met the statutory residency requirements, we executed a formal Claim of Right to Possession to halt the sheriff's post-judgment execution. Outcome: The court accepted the submission, adding Jessica as an individual party defendant, which successfully stopped the immediate lockout and granted her the full right to present her standalone habitability defenses at trial (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Implied Warranty Substantial Breach], [Rent Withholding Justification], [Escrow Account Deposit Verification]. STATUTES: [Cal. Civ. Code § 1941.1]. FACTS: Landlord attempted to evict a tenant for nonpayment, but the tenant successfully established a complete defense by showing a major water leak was left unrepaired for months.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-1288 (Habitability Defense Enforcement): Client Luke Cage withheld his monthly rent payments after a major structural pipe leak caused severe toxic mold growth across his bedroom, which the landlord neglected despite multiple notices (intake notes). The landlord responded by initiating an eviction action based strictly on nonpayment of rent (memo tracking). At trial, we asserted a breach of the implied warranty of habitability, providing verified evidence that the building code violations directly endangered the tenant's health, and confirmed the client had deposited the disputed funds securely inside an escrow account. Outcome: The court ruled the unit was legally uninhabitable, slashed the past-due rent balance by 50%, ordered the landlord to perform comprehensive remediation, and explicitly barred the eviction (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Relocation Benefit Mandates], [No-Fault Just Cause Termination], [Tenant Protection Act Compliance]. STATUTES: [Cal. Civ. Code § 1946.2]. FACTS: Corporate landlord issued a notice to vacate for an owner-move-in but failed to provide the mandatory one-month statutory relocation payment, invalidating the eviction action.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-1311 (No-Fault Relocation Benefit Enforcement): Client Danny Rand received an eviction notice from his landlord citing a no-fault 'just cause' provision under the Tenant Protection Act of 2019 to accommodate an owner-move-in (intake notes). Although Danny had occupied the property for over two years, the landlord refused to supply a relocation payment or waive the final month's rent as strictly mandated by state law (memo tracking). Under Section 1946.2, any no-fault tenancy termination is contextually defective unless the landlord pays an amount exactly equal to one month of the tenant's current rent at the time of notice. Outcome: We filed an answer exposing the lack of statutory compliance; the court agreed that the omission completely invalidated the termination notice and dismissed the unlawful detainer action (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Unnamed Occupant Intervention Window], [Prejudgment Claim Ten-Day Deadline], [Automatic Pleading Requirement]. STATUTES: [Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 415.46]. FACTS: An unnamed subtenant was served with a Prejudgment Claim form alongside the main eviction but failed to file it within ten days, risking a total lockout.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-1402 (Prejudgment Claim Intervention Timeline): Client Matt Murdock discovered that an eviction action was initiated against his roommate, the primary leaseholder, and that a state process server had concurrently served a blank Prejudgment Claim of Right to Possession form on all unnamed occupants (intake notes). Under California eviction rules, unnamed occupants have exactly ten calendar days from the date of service to execute and file this form with the Clerk of Court to protect their independent rights (memo tracking). If they file timely, they automatically become direct defendants in the unlawful detainer action and must submit a formal written answer within five days. Outcome: We successfully filed the Prejudgment Claim on the ninth day along with a fee waiver request, blocking the landlord from executing an immediate building-wide default writ (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Post-Writ Claim Hearing], [Fifteen-Day Rent Escrow Deposit], [Sheriff Lockout Eviction Stop]. STATUTES: [Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 1174.3]. FACTS: Sheriff arrived to execute a writ of possession omitting the tenant's minor aunt; she filed a direct Claim of Right to Possession on the spot.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-1428 (Post-Writ Eviction Stay Challenge): Client Foggy Nelson required an emergency intervention when the county sheriff arrived to execute an eviction lockout against a property where the landlord had neglected to serve a Prejudgment Claim form at the outset (intake notes). Foggy, an unnamed occupant with established long-term residency, filled out a Claim of Right to Possession form and handed it directly to the deputy, which legally forced an immediate halt to the eviction (memo tracking). To sustain the delay, the occupant was required to pay the court filing fee and deposit an amount equal to 15 days' rent within two court days. Outcome: We successfully managed the emergency filing fee process and rent deposit, triggering a mandatory statutory hearing where the court evaluated the validity of his independent tenancy rights (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Lead paint Hazmat Disclosure], [Pre-1978 Housing Warning Mandate], [Rental Agreement Rescission]. STATUTES: [24 C.F.R. § 35.92]. FACTS: Corporate landlord leased a residential unit constructed in 1965 but failed to append a federal lead warning notice or deliver the mandatory awareness pamphlet.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-1455 (Lead-Paint Warning Enforcement): Client Jessica Jones moved into an older multi-family apartment building and later uncovered extensive flaking paint chip hazards around the window sills (intake notes). A historical records check confirmed the residential property was built prior to 1978, meaning the landlord was subject to federal strict disclosure mandates requiring a signed Lead Warning Statement and the distribution of an approved prevention pamphlet (memo tracking). Failure to supply these documents constitutes a material health and safety violation, supporting an affirmative defense of structural unhabitability in state court summary actions. Outcome: We asserted the total absence of federal lead hazard notices as a complete defense in the pending eviction lawsuit, forcing the landlord to dismiss the action and pay for complete professional lead remediation (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Security Deposit Invoice Waiver], [Itemized Deduction Accountings], [Hourly Rate Work Description]. STATUTES: [Cal. Civ. Code § 1950.5(h)]. FACTS: Landlord deducted $1,200 for cleanup from a security deposit but refused to produce invoices, relying on a hidden waiver clause within a digital lease agreement.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-1479 (Security Deposit Deduction Defeat): Client Luke Cage sought the recovery of his $2,500 security deposit after his landlord withheld the bulk of the funds using generic, handwritten line items (intake notes). The landlord claimed that Luke had waived his statutory right to receive physical invoices or receipts under an online lease provision (memo tracking). Under California amendments, any valid waiver of documentation rights must explicitly and substantially recite the full statutory text of Section 1950.5(h)(2), and internal work must itemize exact hours and reasonable rates. Outcome: We demonstrated that the landlord's generic lease clause was legally non-compliant and invalid; the court ruled the waiver ineffective, found the deductions unsubstantiated, and ordered the complete return of the security deposit (case summary).

metadata.preview: TARGETS: [Rent Cap Notice Mandates], [Just Cause Statutory Disclosures], [Termination Font Size Check]. STATUTES: [Cal. Civ. Code § 1946.2]. FACTS: Landlord attempted to execute a standard no-cause eviction against a long-term tenant, but the lease omitted the mandatory 12-point font rent cap warning notice.

case_summary: MATTER GSA-2026-1511 (Rent Cap Disclosure Omission): Client Danny Rand was served with a termination notice intended to end his tenancy after two years of occupancy without any statement of underlying cause (intake notes). Under the Tenant Protection Act, landlords are strictly required to include specific statutory language regarding state rent limits and just cause eviction rules in at least 12-point font directly within the written agreement or as an official addendum (memo tracking). Because the landlord omitted this mandatory disclosure, they were legally barred from circumventing the statutory 'just cause' requirements. Outcome: We filed an explicit motion to dismiss the eviction based on the defective lease paperwork; the court ruled that compliance was mandatory, declared the lease termination void, and entered judgment for the client (case summary).
